questions.question:
Consider two events T and S. Let T' denote the complement of the event T. The probability associated with different events are given as follows:
P(T') = 0.6,  P(S | T) = 0.3,  P(S | T') = 0.6
Then, P(T | S) is _____ (rounded off to two decimal places).

understand.plain: We are given the complement probability P(T') = 0.6 (so P(T) = 0.4) and two conditional probabilities. Using the total probability theorem we compute P(S), then apply Bayes' theorem to find P(T | S). The result P(T | S) = P(S|T)*P(T) / P(S) = 0.12 / 0.48 = 0.25.

keywords.explain: P(S) = P(S|T)*P(T) + P(S|T')*P(T') partitions the probability of S over the two exhaustive, mutually exclusive events T and T'.
P(T|S) = P(S|T)*P(T) / P(S) reverses a conditional probability using the prior P(T) and the likelihood P(S|T).
P(T) = 1 - P(T') allows us to recover the prior probability of T from its complement.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; calculation uses total probability and Bayes' theorem numerically.

given.aim: Find P(T | S) given P(T') = 0.6, P(S | T) = 0.3, P(S | T') = 0.6.

terms.meaning: Prior probabilities of T and its complement; P(T) = 1 - P(T') = 0.4.
terms.connects: These are the weights in the total probability formula.

terms.meaning: Likelihoods of observing S given each hypothesis; 0.3 and 0.6 respectively.
terms.example: P(S | T) = 0.3 means S is less likely when T occurs.
terms.connects: Multiplied by priors in Bayes' theorem to yield the posterior.

terms.meaning: Total probability of S obtained by summing over T and T'.
terms.connects: Acts as the normalising constant in Bayes' theorem.

given.plan: Step 1: recover P(T) = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4. Step 2: use total probability to find P(S) = P(S|T)*P(T) + P(S|T')*P(T'). Step 3: apply Bayes' theorem P(T|S) = P(S|T)*P(T)/P(S).

steps.title: Recover P(T) from its complement
steps.note: The complement rule gives the prior probability of T directly.

steps.title: Compute P(S) via total probability
steps.note: T and T' are exhaustive and mutually exclusive, so they partition the sample space.

questions.formula_note: The total probability theorem computes P(S) as a weighted average over T and T', and Bayes' theorem then reverses the conditioning to give P(T|S).

questions.question:
A fair coin is flipped twice and it is known that at least one tail is observed. The probability of getting two tails is:
A. 1/4
B. 1/3
C. 1/2
D. 3/4

understand.plain: The four equally likely outcomes of two coin flips are HH, HT, TH, TT. The condition 'at least one tail' eliminates HH, leaving three outcomes: HT, TH, TT. Of these, only TT gives two tails. So the conditional probability is 1/3.

keywords.explain: P(A | B) = P(A and B) / P(B) restricts the sample space to event B and asks how much of B is also in A.
keywords.example: P(TT | at least one T) = P(TT) / P(at least one T) = (1/4) / (3/4) = 1/3.

keywords.explain: When a condition is given, we consider only the outcomes consistent with that condition; the new sample space is {HT, TH, TT}.
keywords.example: Three equally likely outcomes remain after eliminating HH.

keywords.explain: 'At least one tail' means one or two tails; its complement is HH (no tails), which has probability 1/4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='8' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='no' opacity='0.15'/><rect x='8' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' fill='none' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='48' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>HH</text><text x='48' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>eliminated</text><text x='48' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'>no tail</text><rect x='102' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='142' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>HT</text><text x='142' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 tail</text><text x='142' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>kept</text><rect x='196' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='236' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>TH</text><text x='236' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 tail</text><text x='236' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>kept</text><rect x='290' y='20' width='80' height='100' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>TT</text><text x='330' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2 tails</text><text x='330' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>target</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four outcome boxes: HH is eliminated (no tail), HT and TH are kept but not the target, TT is kept and is the target event.

given.aim: Find P(TT | at least one tail) for two fair coin flips.

terms.meaning: All equally likely outcomes of two flips: {HH, HT, TH, TT}, each with probability 1/4.
terms.connects: Numerator and denominator of the conditional probability.

terms.term: Event B: at least one tail
terms.meaning: Outcomes with one or more tails: {HT, TH, TT}, so P(B) = 3/4.
terms.connects: Denominator of the conditional probability P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).

terms.meaning: Only the outcome TT; P(A) = 1/4 and P(A and B) = P(TT) = 1/4 since TT satisfies at least one tail.
terms.example: P(TT and at least one T) = 1/4.

given.plan: Use P(TT | at least one T) = P(TT) / P(at least one T) = (1/4) / (3/4) = 1/3. Equivalently, count the 3 outcomes in the reduced space; only 1 of them is TT.

steps.title: List the reduced sample space given at least one tail
steps.apply: HH is eliminated because it has no tail. Three equally likely outcomes remain.
steps.note: Each of the three remaining outcomes has probability 1/4 originally.

steps.title: Identify favourable outcomes (TT) in the reduced space
steps.formula_raw: A and B = {TT},  P(A and B) = 1/4
steps.apply: Only TT gives two tails and also satisfies at least one tail.
steps.note: TT is a subset of B so no additional intersection is needed.

steps.title: Apply the conditional probability formula
steps.formula_raw: P(TT | at least one T) = P(TT) / P(at least one T) = (1/4) / (3/4)
steps.note: Equivalently, 1 out of 3 equally likely outcomes in the reduced space is TT.

solution.result: P(two tails | at least one tail) = 1/3 (Option B)

questions.formula_note: The conditional probability formula P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B) restricts attention to the three outcomes with at least one tail, of which TT is exactly one.

questions.question:
In a population of N families, 30% of the families have three children, 20% of the families have two children and the remaining families have one child. What is the probability that a randomly picked child belongs to a family with two children?
A. 1/5
B. 2/9
C. 3/10
D. 2/3

understand.plain: We need to pick a random child, not a random family. The probability equals the fraction of all children that come from two-child families. For every 10 families: 3 have 3 children (9 children), 2 have 2 children (4 children), and 5 have 1 child (5 children), giving 18 children total. The fraction from two-child families is 4/18 = 2/9.

keywords.explain: Picking a random child is not the same as picking a random family. A child is more likely to come from a larger family because larger families contribute more children.
keywords.example: A three-child family contributes 3 children to the pool; a one-child family contributes only 1.

keywords.explain: Multiply the proportion of families by the number of children per family to find each type's contribution to the total child count.
keywords.example: Two-child families: 20% * 2 = 0.4 units of children per 1 unit of families.

keywords.explain: Divide each family type's child contribution by the total children across all family types to get the probability.
keywords.example: Total children per family: 0.3*3 + 0.2*2 + 0.5*1 = 0.9+0.4+0.5 = 1.8. P(two-child family) = 0.4/1.8 = 2/9.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='15' width='115' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='67' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3-child</text><text x='67' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>30% families</text><text x='67' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>9</text><text x='67' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>children / 10 fam.</text><rect x='142' y='15' width='115' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='199' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2-child</text><text x='199' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>20% families</text><text x='199' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>4</text><text x='199' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>target: 4/18</text><rect x='274' y='15' width='115' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='331' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1-child</text><text x='331' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>50% families</text><text x='331' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>5</text><text x='331' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>children / 10 fam.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three family-type boxes showing children count per 10 families: 3-child families give 9 children, 2-child families give 4 children, 1-child families give 5 children; total 18 children.

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly chosen child belongs to a family with exactly two children.

terms.meaning: 30% of families have 3 children, 20% have 2 children, 50% have 1 child (since 100-30-20=50).
terms.example: In 10 families: 3 three-child, 2 two-child, 5 one-child families.
terms.connects: Used to count how many children each type contributes.

terms.meaning: Each family type contributes (proportion * children-per-family) children to the total pool.

given.plan: Compute total children from each type: 0.3*3 + 0.2*2 + 0.5*1 = 1.8. Children from two-child families = 0.2*2 = 0.4. Probability = 0.4/1.8 = 2/9.

steps.title: Identify the family proportions
steps.apply: In 10 representative families: 3 have 3 kids, 2 have 2 kids, 5 have 1 kid.
steps.note: The remaining 50% of families each have one child.

steps.title: Count total children per 10 families
steps.note: This is the denominator for computing the child-weighted probability.

steps.title: Count children from two-child families
steps.apply: 4 children out of 18 come from two-child families.

steps.title: Compute the probability
steps.apply: P(randomly picked child belongs to a two-child family) = 2/9.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is to count children (not families) from each type; the probability equals the fraction of all children that belong to two-child families.

questions.question:
A bag contains 10 blue marbles, 20 green marbles and 30 red marbles. A marble is drawn from the bag, its colour recorded and it is put back in the bag. This process is repeated 3 times. The probability that no two of the marbles drawn have the same colour is:
A. 1/90
B. 1/6
C. 2/3
D. 1/3

understand.plain: There are 60 marbles total. Each draw is with replacement so draws are independent. We need all three draws to have different colours (one blue, one green, one red in any order). The probability of picking a specific colour on one draw is proportional to its count. We sum over all 3! = 6 orderings of the three colours.

keywords.explain: Returning the marble keeps probabilities constant across draws; successive draws are independent.
keywords.example: P(blue on any draw) = 10/60 = 1/6 regardless of previous draws.

keywords.explain: All three draws must be different colours; this means exactly one draw is blue, one green, one red. There are 3! = 6 orderings.
keywords.example: One ordering: draw 1 blue, draw 2 green, draw 3 red has probability (1/6)*(1/3)*(1/2).

keywords.explain: Probability for one specific ordering = product of individual draw probabilities. Sum over all 6 orderings (all have the same product by symmetry of the formula).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the calculation uses independent draws and multiplication of probabilities.

given.aim: Find P(all three draws have different colours) for 3 draws with replacement from 60 marbles (10 blue, 20 green, 30 red).

terms.connects: Individual draw probabilities that are multiplied for each ordering.

terms.meaning: Three different colours can be arranged in 3! = 6 orderings for the three draws.
terms.example: BGR, BRG, GBR, GRB, RBG, RGB are all 6 orderings.
terms.connects: We sum the probability of each ordering; since all orderings share the same product, multiply by 6.

given.plan: Compute P(blue)*P(green)*P(red) = (10/60)*(20/60)*(30/60) = 1/36 for one fixed ordering. Multiply by 3! = 6 orderings: 6/36 = 1/6.

steps.title: Find individual colour probabilities
steps.apply: Total marbles = 60; each colour probability is its count divided by 60.
steps.note: Draws are with replacement so these probabilities stay constant.

steps.title: Compute probability for one specific ordering
steps.note: Independence of draws (replacement) allows direct multiplication.

steps.title: Multiply by the number of orderings
steps.apply: There are 3! = 6 ways to order three distinct colours across three draws; each has the same probability 1/36.
steps.note: All 6 orderings give the same product because multiplication is commutative.

solution.result: P(no two marbles of the same colour) = 1/6 (Option B)

questions.formula_note: Since draws are independent (with replacement), the probability for each ordering is a product of individual probabilities, and all 3! = 6 orderings have the same product.

questions.question:
A sample space has two events A and B such that probabilities P(A ∩ B) = 1/3, P(A' ∩ B') = 1/3, P(A) = 1/3. What is P(B)?
A. 1/3
B. 2/3
C. 1/2
D. 5/6

understand.plain: We use the complement of (A union B): P(A' ∩ B') = P((A union B)') = 1 - P(A union B). Given P(A' ∩ B') = 1/3, we get P(A union B) = 2/3. Then inclusion-exclusion gives P(B) = P(A union B) - P(A) + P(A ∩ B) = 2/3 - 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3.

keywords.explain: A' ∩ B' = (A union B)', so P(A' ∩ B') = 1 - P(A union B).
P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B), so P(B) = P(A union B) - P(A) + P(A ∩ B).
The event that neither A nor B occurs is the complement of the union A union B.
keywords.example: P(A' ∩ B') is the probability that both A and B fail to occur.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; the solution uses De Morgan's law and inclusion-exclusion algebraically.

given.aim: Find P(B) given P(A ∩ B) = 1/3, P(A' ∩ B') = 1/3, P(A) = 1/3.

terms.meaning: Probability that neither A nor B occurs; by De Morgan equals P((A union B)') = 1 - P(A union B).
terms.connects: Allows us to find P(A union B) without a Venn diagram.

terms.meaning: Probability that A or B or both occur; obtained as 1 - P(A' ∩ B') = 2/3.
terms.connects: Used in inclusion-exclusion to isolate P(B).

terms.meaning: P(B) = P(A union B) - P(A) + P(A ∩ B).

given.plan: Use De Morgan: P(A union B) = 1 - P(A' ∩ B') = 2/3. Then rearrange inclusion-exclusion: P(B) = P(A union B) - P(A) + P(A ∩ B) = 2/3 - 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3.

steps.title: Apply De Morgan's law to find P(A union B)
steps.formula_raw: P(A' ∩ B') = P((A union B)') = 1 - P(A union B)
steps.apply: 1/3 = 1 - P(A union B)  =>  P(A union B) = 2/3.
steps.note: De Morgan's law: the complement of a union is the intersection of complements.

steps.title: Rearrange inclusion-exclusion to isolate P(B)
steps.formula_raw: P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B)  =>  P(B) = P(A union B) - P(A) + P(A ∩ B)
steps.note: Substituting all three known values gives P(B) = 2/3.

questions.formula_note: De Morgan's law converts P(A' ∩ B') to P((A union B)'), and the inclusion-exclusion formula P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B) is rearranged to solve for P(B).

questions.question:
What is the probability that in a randomly chosen group of r people at least three people have the same birthday?
A. 1 - (365 * 364 * ... * (365 - r + 1)) / 365^r
questions.answer: X. The displayed option A gives P(at least two people share a birthday) via the complement of all-distinct birthdays, NOT P(at least three share a birthday). This question is excluded from scoring.

understand.plain: The birthday problem for 'at least two sharing' has a well-known formula using the complement of all-distinct birthdays. However, 'at least three sharing' is strictly harder and does not reduce to a simple closed form in the same way. The given option A is the formula for at least two people sharing a birthday. Because the stated option does not match the stated question, this item is marked as excluded (answer X).

keywords.explain: P(at least two share a birthday) = 1 - 365*364*...*(365-r+1) / 365^r, which is the complement of all r birthdays being distinct.
keywords.example: For r=23, P(at least two share) is approximately 0.5073.

keywords.explain: P(at least three share a birthday) requires inclusion-exclusion over all triples and is not expressible in a single compact product formula.
keywords.example: The at-least-three problem needs to subtract the cases where at most two people share any one birthday.

keywords.explain: P(at least k share) = 1 - P(no k people share), but computing P(no 3 people share) requires summing over all configurations where each birthday appears at most twice.
keywords.example: P(no 3 share) is a sum over multinomial distributions, not a falling factorial divided by 365^r.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; the question is excluded due to a mismatch between the stated problem and the given option.

given.aim: Determine P(at least three people share a birthday) in a group of r people with 365 equally likely birthdays.

terms.meaning: P(all r birthdays distinct) = 365 * 364 * ... * (365 - r + 1) / 365^r, the falling factorial divided by 365^r.
terms.connects: This is the complement of at least two sharing, but NOT of at least three sharing.

terms.meaning: The provided option A equals 1 - P(all distinct), which counts at least two sharing, not at least three sharing.
terms.example: If r=2: option A gives 1 - 364/365 = 1/365 (probability exactly 2 share), but at-least-3 is impossible for r=2.
terms.connects: This mismatch disqualifies the item from the scoring.

terms.meaning: 365 * 364 * ... * (365 - r + 1) = P(365, r) is the number of ordered r-tuples of distinct days.
terms.connects: Used in the standard all-distinct birthday formula.

given.plan: Recognise that option A corresponds to the complement of all-distinct birthdays (i.e., at least two share a birthday). Since the question asks for at least three sharing, there is no matching correct option among those listed. The item is excluded.

steps.title: Write P(all r birthdays distinct)
steps.apply: This counts ordered r-tuples of distinct birthdays divided by all ordered r-tuples.
steps.note: This is the standard birthday-problem complement.

steps.title: Identify what option A actually computes
steps.formula_raw: Option A = 1 - P(all distinct) = P(at least two share a birthday)
steps.apply: Option A = 1 - 365*364*...*(365-r+1)/365^r gives P(at least two share), not P(at least three share).
steps.note: The complement of all-distinct is exactly the event that some pair shares a birthday.

steps.title: Note the mismatch and exclusion
steps.formula_raw: P(at least three share) != 1 - P(all distinct)
steps.apply: No simple closed-form answer matching any of the provided options is correct for the at-least-three version; the item is excluded from scoring.
steps.note: Answer recorded as X to indicate an excluded/erroneous question.

solution.result: Question excluded (answer X): the given option matches at least two sharing, not at least three sharing.

questions.formula_note: The falling factorial P(365, r) counts ordered sequences of distinct birthdays; dividing by 365^r gives the all-distinct probability whose complement is at least two (not three) sharing.
